meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Velocity at Bottom of Rough Wedge

intro.kicker: Topic 22 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Velocity at Bottom of Rough Wedge
intro.description: A body starts from rest at the top of a rough inclined wedge of height h and slides to the bottom. Using the work-energy theorem: the gain in kinetic energy equals the loss in potential energy minus the energy lost to friction (FL, where F = friction force and L = length of incline). The result is: ½mv² = mgh − FL, giving v = √[2(mgh − FL)/m] = √[2gh − 2FL/m]. This is the energy method for finding speed at the bottom of any rough incline.

expectedQuestions.description: NEET direct numerical: given h, m, μ, θ, g, find v at the bottom. Method: F = μmgcosθ; L = h/sinθ; FL = μmgcosθ × h/sinθ = μmgh⋅cotθ. Then v = √[2gh(1 − μcotθ)]. Common conceptual: if μ = tanθ (angle of repose), the block barely moves — verify v → 0. Also: compare rough vs smooth wedge speeds.

timeRequired.description: 20 min for energy-method derivation with F and L in terms of ψ, h, 20 min on NEET MCQs. The derivation integrates work done by friction (W_friction = −FL = −μmgcosθ × h/sinθ) with potential energy loss (mgh). Setting KE gained = PE lost + W_friction gives the formula directly.

difficulty.description: Requires expressing L (incline length) in terms of h and θ: L = h/sinθ. Students who try to use kinematics (v² = u² + 2aS) also get the right answer but must correctly find net acceleration a = g(sinθ − μcosθ). The energy method is more elegant and less error-prone. The trap: confusing h (height) with L (incline length).

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers the work-energy theorem on rough inclines. The formula v = √[2(mgh − FL)/m] is equivalent to AP Physics energy loss on a ramp. The key NEET-specific language is the explicit separation of mgh (potential energy) and FL (work against friction), which NEET tests as a formula to recognise rather than just apply.

weightage.sectionTitle: Velocity at Bottom of Rough Wedge — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears approximately every alternate year; 1 question per appearance.
Tested as numerical (find v) or conceptual (compare rough vs smooth wedge).
When μ = 0 (smooth wedge): v = √(2gh), confirming mgh = ½mv².

strategySteps.title: Apply Work-Energy Theorem
strategySteps.description: Net work = change in KE. ½mv² − 0 = mgh (gravity) − FL (friction loss). So ½mv² = mgh − FL ⇒ v = √[2(mgh − FL)/m]. This is the master formula. F = μmgcosθ; L = h/sinθ (incline length from height h and angle θ).

strategySteps.title: Substitute FL in Terms of h and θ
strategySteps.description: FL = μmgcosθ × h/sinθ = μmgh⋅cotθ = μmgh cosθ/sinθ. So v = √[2gh(1 − μcosθ/sinθ)] = √[2gh(1 − μcotθ)]. Verify: μ = 0 ⇒ v = √(2gh) (smooth wedge); μ = tanθ ⇒ v = 0 (angle of repose).

strategySteps.title: Numerical Application
strategySteps.description: NEET gives h, m, g, μ, θ. Calculate F = μmgcosθ, L = h/sinθ, FL = product, then v = √[(2/m)(mgh − FL)] = √[2gh − 2μgh cosθ/sinθ]. Double-check: FL < mgh (otherwise block doesn’t slide at all).

cards.title: Rough Wedge Velocity Notes
cards.description: Full derivation of v = √[2(mgh − FL)/m], FL = μmgh cotθ substitution, comparison with smooth wedge (v = √(2gh)), angle-of-repose limiting case (v = 0), and 6 NEET-pattern numerical problems.

cards.title: Inclined Plane Energy Summary Sheet
cards.description: Smooth wedge, rough wedge, block with applied force, stopping on incline — all energy-method derivations unified. One-page NEET quick reference for all inclined-plane velocity and stopping problems.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Velocity at Bottom of Rough Wedge

cards.title: Finding Speed at Bottom of Rough Wedge
cards.coreRevision: Body of mass m, rough wedge of height h, angle θ, friction coefficient μ. Incline length L = h/sinθ. Friction force F = μmgcosθ. Work against friction: FL = μmgcosθ × h/sinθ = μmgh⋅cotθ. Energy conservation: ½mv² = mgh − FL. Simplify: ½mv² = mgh − μmgh⋅cotθ = mgh(1 − μcotθ). Therefore: v = √[2gh(1 − μcotθ)].
points: Incline length L = h/sinθ (always express L in terms of h and θ)
Check: μ = 0 ⇒ v = √(2gh); μ = tanθ ⇒ v = 0 (angle of repose)

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on rough wedge energy problems.

gaps.title: Formula v = √[2(mgh − FL)/m] Less Explicit in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics does solve energy problems on rough inclines but usually in terms of W_net = ΔKE, not in the explicit form ½mv² = mgh − FL. NEET specifically requires recognising this formula as a template and substituting FL = μmghcotθ directly. NRI students should practise the substitution L = h/sinθ explicitly.
bullets: L = h/sinθ (incline length from height h and angle θ — key substitution).
Verify with limit cases: smooth (μ = 0) and angle of repose (μ = tanθ, v = 0).

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg slides from rest down a rough incline of height 10 m and angle 30°. If μ = 0.2, the speed at the bottom is (g = 10 m/s², cot30° = √3):
For a rough wedge, which of the following correctly represents the energy equation?
questions.explanation: By conservation of energy: KE gained = PE lost − Energy lost to friction. ½mv² = mgh − FL. Since friction opposes motion (acts up the incline), it does negative work on the block, reducing the speed compared to a smooth wedge. Option A is wrong (would give higher speed); options C and D are physically incorrect.

questions.question: For a smooth wedge (μ = 0) of height h, velocity at bottom is:
If FL = mgh (friction loss equals potential energy), v at bottom is:
Incline length L in terms of height h and angle θ is:
Work done against friction on a rough incline of length L with friction force F is:

items.title: Memorise ½mv² = mgh − FL
items.description: This is the energy-method master formula for velocity at the bottom of a rough incline. KE gained = (PE lost) − (friction energy loss). If FL = 0 (smooth), v = √(2gh). If FL = mgh, v = 0 (block doesn’t reach bottom).

items.title: Express FL using L = h/sinθ
items.description: F = μmgcosθ; L = h/sinθ; FL = μmghcosθ/sinθ = μmghcotθ. Substitute directly into the master formula to get v = √[2gh(1 − μcotθ)].

items.title: Verify with limit cases
items.description: μ = 0: v = √(2gh) (smooth wedge). μ = tanθ: v = 0 (angle of repose — block barely moves). These serve as quick sanity checks in NEET MCQs.

items.question: What is FL in the energy formula?
items.answer: FL is the work done against friction as the block slides down the incline. F = μN = μmgcosθ is the friction force (acting up the incline, perpendicular to the normal). L = h/sinθ is the incline length. So FL = μmgcosθ × h/sinθ = μmghcotθ. This represents the energy dissipated as heat over the entire incline path.

items.question: Can I use kinematics instead of the energy method?
items.answer: Yes. Net acceleration a = g(sinθ − μcosθ) down the incline. Length L = h/sinθ. Then v² = 2aL = 2g(sinθ − μcosθ)(h/sinθ) = 2gh(1 − μcosθ/sinθ) = 2gh(1 − μcotθ). This confirms the energy result. However, the energy method (one step) is faster and preferred in NEET.

items.question: What happens if the incline angle is less than the angle of friction?
items.answer: If θ < arctan(μ) (i.e., μ > tanθ), the block does not slide at all by itself — static friction can fully support the block. The formula gives a negative value under the square root if μ⋅cotθ > 1, indicating the block would stop before reaching the bottom (or never move). In NEET problems, the angle is always given such that the block does slide (sinθ > μcosθ).

grounding_summary: Page 244 presents the Velocity at the Bottom of a Rough Wedge via the energy method. The body starts at the top; potential energy mgh is converted to kinetic energy minus the energy lost to friction FL (where F is friction force and L is the incline length). The equation is half*m*v^2 = mgh - FL, giving v = sqrt(2(mgh-FL)/m).
The textbook provides the derivation in steps: loss of energy due to friction = FL, then by the law of conservation of energy, KE = mgh - FL. This is directly cited in the grounding excerpts below.
grounding_excerpt: By the law of conservation of energy
Loss of energy due to friction

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Work-Energy Theorem on Inclines)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for work-energy theorem applications on rough inclines, foundational for derivation of v = sqrt(2(mgh-FL)/m).

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction — Speed at Bottom of Rough Incline — AskIITians
sources.why_used: Comprehensive NEET/JEE resource confirming energy method derivation and limit cases for rough incline problems.

review.notes: Content grounded on page 244 OCR. Formula half*m*v^2 = mgh - FL derived. FL = mu*m*g*h*cot(theta) substituted. Limit cases (smooth wedge, angle of repose) verified. Kinematics alternative method included in FAQ. topic_number=22, total_topics=22.
